{"action":"created","issue":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/issues/27588","repository_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy","labels_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/issues/27588/labels{/name}","comments_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/issues/27588/comments","events_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/issues/27588/events","html_url":"https://github.com/numpy/numpy/issues/27588","id":2597579473,"node_id":"I_kwDOAA3dP86a0-rR","number":27588,"title":"DISCUSS: About issue of masked array","user":{"login":"fengluoqiuwu","id":163119756,"node_id":"U_kgDOCbkCjA","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/163119756?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/fengluoqiuwu","html_url":"https://github.com/fengluoqiuwu","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/fengluoqiuwu/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/fengluoqiuwu/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/fengluoqiuwu/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/fengluoqiuwu/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/fengluoqiuwu/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/fengluoqiuwu/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/fengluoqiuwu/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/fengluoqiuwu/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/fengluoqiuwu/received_events","type":"User","user_view_type":"public","site_admin":false},"labels":[{"id":6359893,"node_id":"MDU6TGFiZWw2MzU5ODkz","url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/labels/15%20-%20Discussion","name":"15 - Discussion","color":"fef2c0","default":false,"description":null},{"id":16917467,"node_id":"MDU6TGFiZWwxNjkxNzQ2Nw==","url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/labels/component:%20numpy.ma","name":"component: numpy.ma","color":"009800","default":false,"description":"masked arrays"}],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":4,"created_at":"2024-10-18T13:51:16Z","updated_at":"2024-10-21T00:57:15Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"NONE","active_lock_reason":null,"body":"While reading the code and addressing some bugs related to `numpy/ma`, I encountered a few questions:

1. **Filling Values in Masked Arrays**:  
   Do we actually care about the exact fill value in masked arrays, given that they are masked by other values? If not, I believe I can resolve [bug 27580](https://github.com/numpy/numpy/issues/27580) by simply removing the check for `inf`.

2. **Default Fill Value for Masked Arrays**:  
   Currently, the default fill value for masked arrays is defined by [`default_filler`](https://github.com/numpy/numpy/blob/bdc8d4e03181deac5280166aec4188318050570d/numpy/ma/core.py#L167C1-L183C63) for Python data types. However, Python doesn’t have unsigned integer types, so for `np.uint` arrays, the default fill value is stored as `np.int64(999999)`. This causes issues in operations like `copyto(..., casting='samekind')`, as seen in [bug 27580](https://github.com/numpy/numpy/issues/27580) and [bug 27269](https://github.com/numpy/numpy/issues/27269). Should we consider using NumPy data types for the default fill value to ensure that the fill value matches the data type of the array (e.g., using a fill value that corresponds to integers or unsigned integers as appropriate)?

3. **Large Default Fill Values**:  
   Some default fill values seem quite large, such as `999999` for `np.int8` and `1.e20` for `np.float16`. What would be an appropriate default fill value for masked arrays, particularly for small data types like `int8` and `float16`? ([bug25677](https://github.com/numpy/numpy/issues/25677))

4. **Reviewing `copyto` in Masked Arrays**:  
   Should we perform a comprehensive review of `copyto` functionality for masked arrays? It seems likely that similar bugs could exist due to the same root cause.

5. **Testing for Small Data Types**:  
   Should we extend the test suite to include small data types (e.g., `int8` and `float16`) to ensure that functions handle these cases correctly?

6. **Checking Method Consistency**
   Should we check the consistency of method between (no-masked) `masked array` and `ndarray`? There is some difference between methods and behaviors of (no-masked) `masked array` and `ndarray`, for example, see [bug27258](https://github.com/numpy/numpy/issues/27258).

7. **Making Standard Clear**
   Some methods' standard is not clear. For example, should we auto mask the invalid result? In some function (such as `sqrt` , `std`) it does, but in other function (such as `median`, `mean`). Something more worse is that in the document some function don't mention it but auto change the mask (`sqrt` [`std`](https://numpy.org/devdocs/reference/generated/numpy.ma.masked_array.std.html#numpy.ma.masked_array.std)) , and others do mention it but not change ([`mean`](https://numpy.org/devdocs/reference/generated/numpy.ma.mean.html#numpy.ma.mean)).
   And something more worse is that, some important methods don't have clear explanation both in document and doc string, some of them are really important. For example, `__array_wrap__` , most of the callings to ufunc call it, and I think it might be the cause of the [bug25635](https://github.com/numpy/numpy/issues/25635).

Since I'm not sure where to place these questions, I’ve marked this as a discussion for now.

","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/issues/27588/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/issues/27588/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":null},"comment":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/issues/comments/2425346160","html_url":"https://github.com/numpy/numpy/issues/27588#issuecomment-2425346160","issue_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/issues/27588","id":2425346160,"node_id":"IC_kwDOAA3dP86Qj9hw","user":{"login":"fengluoqiuwu","id":163119756,"node_id":"U_kgDOCbkCjA","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/163119756?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/fengluoqiuwu","html_url":"https://github.com/fengluoqiuwu","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/fengluoqiuwu/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/fengluoqiuwu/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/fengluoqiuwu/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/fengluoqiuwu/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/fengluoqiuwu/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/fengluoqiuwu/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/fengluoqiuwu/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/fengluoqiuwu/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/fengluoqiuwu/received_events","type":"User","user_view_type":"public","site_admin":false},"created_at":"2024-10-21T00:57:14Z","updated_at":"2024-10-21T00:57:14Z","author_association":"NONE","body":"> I think that the fundamental issue with masked arrays is that np.ma.MaskedArray is a subclass of np.ndarray, but it alters the behavior of the base class. Consequently, any routine that works with np.ndarray will work with MaskedArray inputs formally, but may potentially break its semantics. To correctly maintain the semantics, every routine would need to test and specialize for the input type. This seems simply impossible.
> Therefore, I suspect it is fundamentally impossible to ensure that MaskedArray works predictably in general.

I understand that it's not feasible to test all NumPy methods comprehensively for every scenario. However, I believe that, within the current architecture of masked arrays, it would still be valuable to focus on testing certain key areas. For instance, `__array_wrap__` is a critical function since many of NumPy's general methods (such as mathematical functions) call it. Ensuring that it behaves correctly would be a good starting point. 

Additionally, I think it’s important to verify that the functions in `np.ma` behave as expected. This makes sense because `np.ma` is specifically designed for masked arrays. And perhaps making sure that when a masked array without a mask is passed, the results align with those from NumPy's general functions.

Particularly, I believe that within the current masked array, we might want to focus on testing edge cases like `np.uint8` (since it stores the fill value as `np.int64(999999)`) and `np.float16` (where the default fill value may fall outside of the type's range). We should also look into testing methods that involve type conversions or affect `fill_value`. While I understand that exhaustive testing may not be practical, perhaps maintaining a minimal set of core tests would be sufficient. More comprehensive testing could be reserved for a specific file that isn’t run by default, allowing developers to use it when modifying functions.

That said, I do think it's important to ensure that most methods work reliably under most conditions, and that at least in a certain version, we have a set of tests that have passed.

1. **Doxygen Comments**: Added detailed Doxygen-style comments to improve code documentation and enhance readability based on my understanding of the code. And I use `@internal` to show an object is internal, and `@file_internal` to show that it is file internal(should only be used in current file).
2. **Naming Conventions**: Modified some function names according to the [C Style Guide](https://numpy.org/neps/nep-0045-c_style_guide.html) for better clarity and consistency.
3. **Macro Modifications**: Updated the definitions of macros, which may help prevent potential conflicts or issues.
 - Delete undefinition of `SHIFT_OVERFLOW` and `NOT_FOUND`, since I couldn't found their definition both in the hole project and the PR which they are added to Numpy.
 - Add undefinition of `MAX_SHIFT` as I couldn't find it to be used or undefined elsewhere.

In my personal view, some of these changes might be overly detailed (given that this is my first time reading the code), and the modifications could potentially introduce security concerns. Therefore, I present these changes as suggestions for reference. I would be very pleased if they contribute to improving the documentation of the code.

1. **Doxygen Comments**: Added detailed Doxygen-style comments to improve code documentation and enhance readability based on my understanding of the code. And I use `@internal` to show an object is internal, and `@file_internal` to show that it is file internal(should only be used in current file).
2. **Naming Conventions**: Modified some function names according to the [C Style Guide](https://numpy.org/neps/nep-0045-c_style_guide.html) for better clarity and consistency.
3. **Macro Modifications**: Updated the definitions of macros, which may help prevent potential conflicts or issues.
 - Delete undefinition of `SHIFT_OVERFLOW` and `NOT_FOUND`, since I couldn't found their definition both in the hole project and the PR which they are added to Numpy.
 - Add undefinition of `MAX_SHIFT` as I couldn't find it to be used or undefined elsewhere.

In my personal view, some of these changes might be overly detailed (given that this is my first time reading the code), and the modifications could potentially introduce security concerns. Therefore, I present these changes as suggestions for reference. I would be very pleased if they contribute to improving the documentation of the code.

If there isn't an open issue about what you suspect is a flakey test, go ahead and open an issue.

Thank you for answering my questions. I just joined NumPy, so I’m not very familiar with this aspect.

> otally separately to this PR, I'd like to give you a little feedback on your contribution style. First, It looks like you're trying to open a lot of PRs. Just a head's up that our review capacity, especially related to masked arrays, is pretty minimal right now, so opening a lot of PRs without waiting for existing PRs to be reviewed is probably counterproductive.

Considering this, I will prioritize submitting PRs that involve minor modifications and bug fixes in the future. Recently, I've reviewed the code related to masked arrays, searched through most of the related issues, and fixed some of them, which I believe is the main reason I submitted so many PRs lately. Additionally, is there a way to label a PR or issue as not particularly urgent?

> One thing that would help and also give us signals that you want to help out the project in general is to try looking at other PRs and giving code review there. A rule of thumb I have in my head is that for every PR I open, I give code review on another PR. It's a common misconception that only maintainers can give code review. That's not true, anyone can spot bugs and issues. Just try to keep code review comments to things you're pretty sure are bugs in PRs and keep in mind that NumPy has plenty of preexisting issues and PRs shouldn't get bogged down in filibustering.

I have attempted to look at other PRs, but I'm quite unfamiliar with most of the changes they involve. I am currently reading the low-level C source code of NumPy to understand how it operates.

> Finally, please feel free to come to either the weekly community meeting or triage meeting we have on zoom. Those meetings are a great opportunity to introduce yourself to PR reviewers and reduce communication latency.

Lastly, I would like to express that I subjectively wish to attend the weekly community or triage meetings; however, due to time zone differences and my very poor English skills, I regret that I cannot participate.


Thank you once again for your guidance!

{"action":"created","issue":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/issues/26899","repository_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy","labels_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/issues/26899/labels{/name}","comments_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/issues/26899/comments","events_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/issues/26899/events","html_url":"https://github.com/numpy/numpy/issues/26899","id":2399954882,"node_id":"I_kwDOAA3dP86PDGfC","number":26899,"title":"BUG: Cannot convert masked array of python datetimes to datetime64 after changing fill value","user":{"login":"ArthurGW","id":26261951,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjI2MjYxOTUx","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/26261951?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/ArthurGW","html_url":"https://github.com/ArthurGW","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/ArthurGW/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/ArthurGW/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/ArthurGW/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/ArthurGW/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/ArthurGW/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/ArthurGW/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/ArthurGW/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/ArthurGW/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/ArthurGW/received_events","type":"User","user_view_type":"public","site_admin":false},"labels":[{"id":6359214,"node_id":"MDU6TGFiZWw2MzU5MjE0","url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/labels/00%20-%20Bug","name":"00 - Bug","color":"fef2c0","default":false,"description":null},{"id":81818822,"node_id":"MDU6TGFiZWw4MTgxODgyMg==","url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/labels/57%20-%20Close?","name":"57 - Close?","color":"207de5","default":false,"description":"Issues which may be closable unless discussion continued"}],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":6,"created_at":"2024-07-10T07:17:38Z","updated_at":"2024-10-22T03:37:53Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"NONE","active_lock_reason":null,"body":"### Describe the issue:

When creating a masked array with object dtype, with the init argument `fill_value=datetime.datetime.max` (for example), then later changing the fill value to a different datetime value, calling `broken.astype('datetime64[...]')` results in an error converting the fill value to the new dtype. (This is also the case if you re-set exactly the same fill value.)

Internally, the fill value you pass in ends up wrapped in two layers of numpy.array, i.e. `broken._fill_value`  == `array(array(datetime.datetime(1, 1, 1, 0, 0), dtype=object), dtype=object)`. This object cannot be converted.

I suspect this would happen for other object arrays, since the code that wraps the fill value in an array to make it a numpy scalar doesn't do anything specific with datetimes.

The error message you get is a bit misleading, "TypeError: Cannot convert fill_value 0001-01-01 00:00:00 to dtype datetime64[s]", because it doesn't show that it's actually wrapped in arrays. Took me a while to puzzle over that one!

There is a workaround: instead of _changing_ the fill value on the MA, create a new MA: `numpy.ma.array(broken, fill_value=datetime.datetime.min).astype('datetime64[...])` works. I don't know if this results in any inefficient copying though.

### Reproduce the code example:

```python
import numpy as np

# This array converts fine
works = numpy.ma.array([datetime.datetime.now(), None],
                       mask=[False, True], 
                       fill_value=datetime.datetime.min)

works.fill_value  # output: datetime.datetime(1, 1, 1, 0, 0)
works._fill_value  # output: array(datetime.datetime(1, 1, 1, 0, 0), dtype=object)
works.astype('datetime64[s]')  # works

# This doesn't after setting `broken.fill_value`
broken = numpy.ma.array([datetime.datetime.now(), None],
                        mask=[False, True],
                        fill_value=datetime.datetime.max)

broken.fill_value = datetime.datetime.min  # This breaks things
broken.fill_value  # output: array(datetime.datetime(1, 1, 1, 0, 0), dtype=object) - note the extra array/scalar wrapper around the object
broken._fill_value  # output: array(array(datetime.datetime(1, 1, 1, 0, 0), dtype=object), dtype=object) - note the double array wrapper
broken.astype('datetime64[s]')  # fails
```


### Error message:

```shell
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "...\lib\site-packages\numpy\ma\core.py", line 462, in _check_fill_value
    fill_value = np.array(fill_value, copy=False, dtype=ndtype)
ValueError: Could not convert object to NumPy datetime

The above exception was the direct cause of the following exception:

Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "...\lib\site-packages\numpy\ma\core.py", line 3062, in __array_finalize__
    self._fill_value = _check_fill_value(self._fill_value, self.dtype)
  File "...\lib\site-packages\numpy\ma\core.py", line 468, in _check_fill_value
    raise TypeError(err_msg % (fill_value, ndtype)) from e
TypeError: Cannot convert fill_value 0001-01-01 00:00:00 to dtype datetime64[s]
```


### Python and NumPy Versions:

1.21.6
3.10.6 (tags/v3.10.6:9c7b4bd, Aug  1 2022, 21:53:49) [MSC v.1932 64 bit (AMD64)]

### Runtime Environment:

However, there are two basic issues causing this problem, and it seems to stem from `ndarray`, not specifically from the masked array.

The first issue relates to `dtype`. It appears that `ndarray` does not recognize `datetime` and treats it as `object` unless specified explicitly by parameters. For example:

```python
>>> import numpy as np
>>> import datetime

>>> print(f"{np.array(1)=}")                     # works with int
np.array(1)=array(1)
>>> print(f"{np.array(datetime.datetime.min)=}")  # fails with datetime
np.array(datetime.datetime.min)=array(datetime.datetime(1, 1, 1, 0, 0), dtype=object)  
>>> print(f"{np.array(datetime.datetime.min,dtype=np.datetime64)=}")   # works if I give it dtype
np.array(datetime.datetime.min,dtype=np.datetime64)=array('0001-01-01T00:00:00.000000', dtype='datetime64[us]')
```

The second issue pertains to scalar access, which behaves unexpectedly with objects that are not part of NumPy (or perhaps there are some misunderstandings on my part):

```python
>>> fill_value = np.array(1)
>>> fill_value[()] = np.array(1)
>>> fill_value                                           # works with int type
array(1)

>>> class A:
>>>     def __init__(self):
>>>         self.a = 0
>>> fill_value = np.array(A())
>>> fill_value[()] = np.array(A())
>>> fill_value                                           # fails with any object type
array(array(<__main__.A object at 0x00000132D5E4A0C0>, dtype=object), dtype=object)
```
I haven't learned enough about C code yet, so I'm unable to address these issues myself. I hope someone else can take a look and help with a fix.","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/issues/comments/2428142481/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"performed_via_github_app":null}}

Enhancement","color":"fef2c0","default":false,"description":null}],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":2,"created_at":"2024-10-21T14:48:02Z","updated_at":"2024-10-22T06:32:35Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"NONE","active_lock_reason":null,"draft":false,"pull_request":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/pulls/27612","html_url":"https://github.com/numpy/numpy/pull/27612","diff_url":"https://github.com/numpy/numpy/pull/27612.diff","patch_url":"https://github.com/numpy/numpy/pull/27612.patch","merged_at":null},"body":"This pull request includes the following changes:

1. **Doxygen Comments**: Added detailed Doxygen-style comments to improve code documentation and enhance readability based on my understanding of the code. And I use `@internal` to show an object is internal, and `@file_internal` to show that it is file internal(should only be used in current file).
2. **Naming Conventions**: Modified some function names according to the [C Style Guide](https://numpy.org/neps/nep-0045-c_style_guide.html) for better clarity and consistency.
3. **Macro Modifications**: Updated the definitions of macros, which may help prevent potential conflicts or issues.
 - Delete undefinition of `SHIFT_OVERFLOW` and `NOT_FOUND`, since I couldn't found their definition both in the hole project and the PR which they are added to Numpy.
 - Add undefinition of `MAX_SHIFT` as I couldn't find it to be used or undefined elsewhere.

In my personal view, some of these changes might be overly detailed (given that this is my first time reading the code), and the modifications could potentially introduce security concerns. Therefore, I present these changes as suggestions for reference. I would be very pleased if they contribute to improving the documentation of the code.","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/issues/27612/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/issues/27612/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":null},"comment":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/issues/comments/2428370850","html_url":"https://github.com/numpy/numpy/pull/27612#issuecomment-2428370850","issue_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/issues/27612","id":2428370850,"node_id":"IC_kwDOAA3dP86Qvf-i","user":{"login":"fengluoqiuwu","id":163119756,"node_id":"U_kgDOCbkCjA","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/163119756?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/fengluoqiuwu","html_url":"https://github.com/fengluoqiuwu","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/fengluoqiuwu/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/fengluoqiuwu/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/fengluoqiuwu/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/fengluoqiuwu/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/fengluoqiuwu/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/fengluoqiuwu/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/fengluoqiuwu/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/fengluoqiuwu/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/fengluoqiuwu/received_events","type":"User","user_view_type":"public","site_admin":false},"created_at":"2024-10-22T06:32:34Z","updated_at":"2024-10-22T06:32:34Z","author_association":"NONE","body":"> I also am aware that someone is trying to change this code upstream: https://discuss.python.org/t/request-for-review-gh-119702-new-dynamic-algorithm-selection-for-string-search-rfind-alignment/58047

Thank you for the clarification regarding the synchronization with CPython. I understand now why the definitions for `SHIFT_OVERFLOW` and `NOT_FOUND` were not found. They were indeed included in the [commit](https://github.com/python/cpython/commit/73a85c4e1da42db28e3de57c868d24a089b8d277#diff-67ae1b84396bc0119774489a3e62be151a8e1b1317a63c540f976d57d3e0489dR290) but were removed without corresponding `#undef` statements. Regarding `MAX_SHIFT`, I could not find the `#undef` in the original PR, and I believe it is unlikely that users will need this.

Additionally, it seems we may have overlooked adding the `MEMCHR_CUT_OFF` `#undef` when copying from upstream. 

> This code comes from CPython and I think we want to keep it synced with upstream.

However, there are still some differences between our version and the upstream code, and I'm not entirely sure why that is. It could be due to modifications we've made during usage or changes in the upstream update. One thing is clear: the current documentation does not suit our version, and we lack adequate documentation. I believe it's important to add documentation—whether by drafting it ourselves or by adapting their documentation text to fit our context.","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/issues/comments/2428370850/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"performed_via_github_app":null}}

When we run the following test code:

```python
Copy code
>>> import numpy as np
>>> a = np.ma.masked_array(1, mask=True)
>>> type(a)
>>> np.sqrt(a)
```
We get:

```python
Copy code
masked_array(data=1.,
             mask=False,
       fill_value=1e+20) # current code

masked_array(data=--,
             mask=True,
       fill_value=1e+20,
            dtype=float64) # PR code
```
So, I'm not sure if I'm misunderstanding the issue. Is this the expected result?

While it does raise a runtime warning:
```python
Copy code
<ipython-input-3-491a5a0a5603>:1: DeprecationWarning: __array_wrap__ must accept context and return_scalar arguments (positionally) in the future. (Deprecated NumPy 2.0)
  np.sqrt(np.ma.masked)
```

This warning is also present in the current implementation, and I think it may be a separate issue unrelated to the changes proposed in this PR.","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/issues/comments/2431171712/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"performed_via_github_app":null}}

Doing this will (hopefully, I don't recall the exact code), tell you more about what is going on. The fact is we try to pass `context`, and incorrectly, give up on it. It may be that the error matching in the C code is too fuzzy or something else is off. But even in that case there is at least a `TypeError` being raised somewhere and that in itself seems off.

It seems we're encountering another type issue. The method `domain` returns an array of np.float64, and when using the bitwise OR operator (`|`) with another array, it raises a runtime warning.

```python
Copy code
np.copyto(result, fill_value, where=d)

# Update the mask
if m is nomask:
    m = d
else:
    # Avoid modifying in place to prevent back-propagation
    m = (m | d)  <----- There
```
I'm also curious about how this results in an error indicating that the context is None... pull request includes the following changes:

1. **Doxygen Comments**: Added detailed Doxygen-style comments to improve code documentation and enhance readability based on my understanding of the code. And I use `@internal` to show an object is internal, and `@file_internal` to show that it is file internal(should only be used in current file).
2. **Naming Conventions**: Modified some function names according to the [C Style Guide](https://numpy.org/neps/nep-0045-c_style_guide.html) for better clarity and consistency.
3. **Macro Modifications**: Updated the definitions of macros, which may help prevent potential conflicts or issues.
 - Delete undefinition of `SHIFT_OVERFLOW` and `NOT_FOUND`, since I couldn't found their definition both in the hole project and the PR which they are added to Numpy.
 - Add undefinition of `MAX_SHIFT` as I couldn't find it to be used or undefined elsewhere.

In my personal view, some of these changes might be overly detailed (given that this is my first time reading the code), and the modifications could potentially introduce security concerns. Therefore, I present these changes as suggestions for reference. I would be very pleased if they contribute to improving the documentation of the Documentation","color":"fef2c0","default":false,"description":""}],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":1,"created_at":"2024-10-23T16:09:43Z","updated_at":"2024-10-23T19:41:28Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"NONE","active_lock_reason":null,"body":"### Issue with current documentation:

The documentation example for `numpy.ndarray.byteswap` demonstrates viewing an array of integers as `np.uint8`. However, the behavior of the example is platform-dependent and may lead to confusion, especially for users on 64-bit Windows systems where the default dtype for `np.array` is `np.int32` rather than `np.int64`.

**Steps to Reproduce:**
1. On a 64-bit Windows system, run the following code:
```python
import numpy as np
A = np.array([1, 2, 3])
print(A.view(np.uint8))
```
2. The output on 64-bit Windows systems shows the default dtype as `np.int32`:
```
array([1, 0, 0, 0, 2, 0, 0, 0, 3, 0, 0, 0], dtype=uint8)
```
This contrasts with the documentation example, which assumes `np.int64`:
```
array([1, 0, 0, 0, 0, 0, 0, 0, 2, 0, 0, 0, 0, 0, 0, 0, 3, 0, 0, 0, 0, 0, 0, 0], dtype=uint8)
```
**Problem Description:**
The discrepancy between the documentation and actual behavior on different platforms could lead to confusion for users. The example appears to assume that `np.array` defaults to `np.int64`, which is true on most 64-bit Linux systems but not on 64-bit Windows, where `np.int32` is the default dtype. This difference is not mentioned in the documentation.


### Idea or request for content:

- Explicitly set the dtype in the example (e.g., `np.array([1, 2, 3], dtype=np.int64)`) to ensure consistency across platforms.
- Alternatively, add a note explaining that the default dtype of `np.array` may vary by platform (`np.int64` on most 64-bit Linux systems, `np.int32` on Windows).

This clarification will help avoid confusion for users on different platforms.","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/issues/27628/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/issues/27628/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":null},"comment":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/issues/comments/2433287161","html_url":"https://github.com/numpy/numpy/issues/27628#issuecomment-2433287161","issue_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/issues/27628","id":2433287161,"node_id":"IC_kwDOAA3dP86RCQP5","user":{"login":"fengluoqiuwu","id":163119756,"node_id":"U_kgDOCbkCjA","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/163119756?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/fengluoqiuwu","html_url":"https://github.com/fengluoqiuwu","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/fengluoqiuwu/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/fengluoqiuwu/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/fengluoqiuwu/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/fengluoqiuwu/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/fengluoqiuwu/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/fengluoqiuwu/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/fengluoqiuwu/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/fengluoqiuwu/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/fengluoqiuwu/received_events","type":"User","user_view_type":"public","site_admin":false},"created_at":"2024-10-23T19:41:27Z","updated_at":"2024-10-23T19:41:27Z","author_association":"NONE","body":"I ran the code on my 64-bit Windows machine, and it produced the expected result:

Documentation","color":"fef2c0","default":false,"description":""}],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":3,"created_at":"2024-10-23T16:09:43Z","updated_at":"2024-10-23T20:13:48Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"NONE","active_lock_reason":null,"body":"### Issue with current documentation:

The documentation example for `numpy.ndarray.byteswap` demonstrates viewing an array of integers as `np.uint8`. However, the behavior of the example is platform-dependent and may lead to confusion, especially for users on 64-bit Windows systems where the default dtype for `np.array` is `np.int32` rather than `np.int64`.

**Steps to Reproduce:**
1. On a 64-bit Windows system, run the following code:
```python
import numpy as np
A = np.array([1, 2, 3])
print(A.view(np.uint8))
```
2. The output on 64-bit Windows systems shows the default dtype as `np.int32`:
```
array([1, 0, 0, 0, 2, 0, 0, 0, 3, 0, 0, 0], dtype=uint8)
```
This contrasts with the documentation example, which assumes `np.int64`:
```
array([1, 0, 0, 0, 0, 0, 0, 0, 2, 0, 0, 0, 0, 0, 0, 0, 3, 0, 0, 0, 0, 0, 0, 0], dtype=uint8)
```
**Problem Description:**
The discrepancy between the documentation and actual behavior on different platforms could lead to confusion for users. The example appears to assume that `np.array` defaults to `np.int64`, which is true on most 64-bit Linux systems but not on 64-bit Windows, where `np.int32` is the default dtype. This difference is not mentioned in the documentation.


### Idea or request for content:

- Explicitly set the dtype in the example (e.g., `np.array([1, 2, 3], dtype=np.int64)`) to ensure consistency across platforms.

I believe this is the correct approach, but I'm not entirely confident. The method `__array_wrap__` lacks both a proper explanation in the [documentation](https://numpy.org/devdocs/reference/generated/numpy.ma.MaskedArray.__array_wrap__.html#numpy.ma.MaskedArray.__array_wrap__) and in the code itself, which greatly impedes understanding and thorough testing.

```python
    def __array_wrap__(self, obj, context=None, return_scalar=False):
        """
        Special hook for ufuncs.

        Wraps the numpy array and sets the mask according to context.

        """
```

That said, the bug has been fixed with this PR, and all tests have passed on my computer. However, to be honest, I remain concerned about this method since many other methods call it. It could potentially lead to issues if not handled carefully.

{"action":"opened","number":27642,"pull_request":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/pulls/27642","id":2145772385,"node_id":"PR_kwDOAA3dP85_5eNh","html_url":"https://github.com/numpy/numpy/pull/27642","diff_url":"https://github.com/numpy/numpy/pull/27642.diff","patch_url":"https://github.com/numpy/numpy/pull/27642.patch","issue_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/issues/27642","number":27642,"state":"open","locked":false,"title":"BUG: Add complex support to MaskedArray domain","user":{"login":"fengluoqiuwu","id":163119756,"node_id":"U_kgDOCbkCjA","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/163119756?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/fengluoqiuwu","html_url":"https://github.com/fengluoqiuwu","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/fengluoqiuwu/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/fengluoqiuwu/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/fengluoqiuwu/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/fengluoqiuwu/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/fengluoqiuwu/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/fengluoqiuwu/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/fengluoqiuwu/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/fengluoqiuwu/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/fengluoqiuwu/received_events","type":"User","user_view_type":"public","site_admin":false},"body":"## Add Complex Support to MaskedArray Domain
This PR introduces complex number support to the MaskedArray domain. In the original implementation, some universal functions (ufuncs), such as sqrt, relied solely on real-domain checks. This limitation could lead to undefined results when these ufuncs were applied to complex numbers.

To address this issue, we have added complex-domain checks to the relevant ufuncs within the MaskedArray. This enhancement ensures that complex numbers are handled correctly, providing more reliable results when performing operations on masked arrays that include complex data.

fixes #19923 Bug","color":"fef2c0","default":false,"description":null},{"id":16917467,"node_id":"MDU6TGFiZWwxNjkxNzQ2Nw==","url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/labels/component:%20numpy.ma","name":"component: numpy.ma","color":"009800","default":false,"description":"masked arrays"}],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":2,"created_at":"2021-05-10T13:49:02Z","updated_at":"2024-10-26T12:25:01Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"NONE","active_lock_reason":null,"body":"<!-- Please describe the issue in detail here, and fill in the fields below -->

The `MaskedArray` class has unexpected special-casing of `ndarray` objects within an object array. I expect an object array to be a generic container of whatever is in each cell, and if that element is masked then accessing it should always return `np.ma.masked`. Instead `MaskedArray` appears to turn the `ndarray` into a fully-masked `MaskedArray`.

### Reproducing code example:

<!-- A short code example that reproduces the problem/missing feature. It should be
self-contained, i.e., possible to run as-is via 'python myproblem.py' -->

```python
import numpy as np
>>> m = np.ma.array([None, [1, 2], np.array([1, 2])], dtype=object)
>>> m
masked_array(data=[None, list([1, 2]), array([1, 2])],
             mask=False,
       fill_value='?',
            dtype=object)
>>> m.mask = [True, True, True]
>>> m[0]
masked
>>> m[1]
masked
>>> m[2]  # Accessing the ndarray has auto-magically converted it to masked_array
masked_array(data=[--, --],
             mask=[ True,  True],
       fill_value=999999,
            dtype=int64)
>>> m.data[2]  # The data element under the mask is still the original ndarray
array([1, 2])
```

Expected:
```
>>> m[2]
masked
```

### Error message:
N/A

Perhaps it won't be treated as "enhancement" after this :)

Regarding the bug, it indeed seems related to the `dtype` modification section and likely unrelated to the original issue. Here’s a possible fix to address it:
```diff
    @dtype.setter
    def dtype(self, dtype):
        super(MaskedArray, type(self)).dtype.__set__(self, dtype)
        if self._mask is not nomask:
            self._mask = self._mask.view(make_mask_descr(dtype), ndarray)
            # Try to reset the shape of the mask (if we don't have a void).
            # This raises a ValueError if the dtype change won't work.
+           try:
-                self._mask.shape = self.shape
-         except (AttributeError, TypeError):
-                pass
+            if not self._mask.size==1:
+                try:
+                    self._mask.shape = self.shape
+                except (AttributeError, TypeError):
+                    pass
```

The mask in an array can indeed remain compact as `True` or `False`, so reshaping _mask is unnecessary in these cases. This should simplify the handling and avoid unnecessary reshaping attempts.","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/issues/comments/2439591136/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"performed_via_github_app":null}}

```

Bug","color":"fef2c0","default":false,"description":null},{"id":16917467,"node_id":"MDU6TGFiZWwxNjkxNzQ2Nw==","url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/labels/component:%20numpy.ma","name":"component: numpy.ma","color":"009800","default":false,"description":"masked arrays"}],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":3,"created_at":"2019-09-09T12:55:24Z","updated_at":"2024-10-26T16:41:41Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"NONE","active_lock_reason":null,"body":"When passing an array with data type `float32` into `np.ma.average` there is a difference in the data type of the returned result depending on whether the input array is masked or not. In the case where the input data is a `MaskedArray`, the data type of the result is `float64`. If the input data in not a `MaskedArray`, the data type of the result is `float32`. 

This seems to relate the the information in the docstrings for `np.ma.average` (https://docs.scipy.org/doc/numpy-1.17.0/reference/generated/numpy.ma.average.html) and `np.average` (https://docs.scipy.org/doc/numpy-1.17.0/reference/generated/numpy.average.html), which describe different behaviour relating to the data type of the output array.

Is there any scope to unify the behaviour of these two functions? It would be helpful if the data type of the output arrays were consistent.

The same behaviour also exists for `np.var` and `np.ma.var`

### Reproducing code example:

<!-- A short code example that reproduces the problem/missing feature. It should be
self-contained, i.e., possible to run as-is via 'python myproblem.py' -->

```python
import numpy as np

data = np.ones((3,3,3), dtype=np.float32)
mean_unmasked = np.ma.average(data, axis=0)
print(mean_unmasked.dtype) # Prints float32

data_masked = np.ma.MaskedArray(
    np.ones((3,3,3), dtype=np.float32), 
    np.ones((3,3,3), dtype=bool))
masked_average = np.ma.average(data_masked, axis=0)
print(masked_average.dtype) # Prints float64
```

### Numpy/Python version information:
numpy versions tested: 1.15.4 and 1.17.0

Programmers will differ in what they regard as suitable names.

For some names, I simply added an underscore (`_`) to align with our C-style guide. For names with more substantial changes, I felt the original names could be somewhat misleading. ```python

numpy.ma.average(mymaskedarray2)
numpy.ma.average(mymaskedarray)

print(type(numpy.ma.average(mymaskedarray)))

```

>>> import numpy as np

I also see similar warnings with greater_equal, less_equal, and absolute in a call to matplotlib symlog:

```

Windows 7 x64
Anaconda 4.3.1+ (custom to bring in np 1.12)
Python 2.7.13
numpy 1.12.1

This may be related to #8230, which suggests this is unique to Windows and Python 2.7, and #7440. The latter suggests it is a 32-bit problem but my environment is 64-bit. I don't think this was an issue with an earlier version of numpy, but I'm not sure of the prior version I used. Bug","color":"fef2c0","default":false,"description":null},{"id":16917467,"node_id":"MDU6TGFiZWwxNjkxNzQ2Nw==","url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/labels/component:%20numpy.ma","name":"component: numpy.ma","color":"009800","default":false,"description":"masked arrays"}],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":1,"created_at":"2017-02-26T13:58:18Z","updated_at":"2024-10-27T07:06:35Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"MEMBER","active_lock_reason":null,"body":"Right now, the default arguments for sorting-related methods are:

Defaults to `axis=-1`:
 * `np.sort`
 * `np.ndarray.sort`
 * `np.argsort`
 * `np.ndarray.argsort`
 * `np.ma.sort`
 * `np.masked_array.sort`
 * The _documentation_ of `np.ma.argsort`
 * The _documentation_ of `np.masked_array.argsort`

Defaults to `axis=None`:
 * The _implementation_ of `np.ma.argsort`
 * The _implementation_ of `np.masked_array.argsort`

For 1D arrays, there is no difference, as `None` just means flatten and use the only axis.

For 2D arrays, however, code written for `ndarrays` can fail in unexpected ways when passed a `masked_array`, because `argsort` breaks liskov subsitution:

```
def foo(x):
    i = x.argsort()
    assert(i.shape == x.shape)

>>> foo(som_arr)  # ok
>>> foo(som_arr.view(MaskedArray)) # AssertionError
```","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/issues/8701/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/issues/8701/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":null},"comment":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/issues/comments/2439883044","html_url":"https://github.com/numpy/numpy/issues/8701#issuecomment-2439883044","issue_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/issues/8701","id":2439883044,"node_id":"IC_kwDOAA3dP86Rbakk","user":{"login":"fengluoqiuwu","id":163119756,"node_id":"U_kgDOCbkCjA","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/163119756?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/fengluoqiuwu","html_url":"https://github.com/fengluoqiuwu","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/fengluoqiuwu/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/fengluoqiuwu/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/fengluoqiuwu/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/fengluoqiuwu/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/fengluoqiuwu/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/fengluoqiuwu/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/fengluoqiuwu/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/fengluoqiuwu/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/fengluoqiuwu/received_events","type":"User","user_view_type":"public","site_admin":false},"created_at":"2024-10-27T07:06:33Z","updated_at":"2024-10-27T07:06:33Z","author_association":"NONE","body":"It seems that this warning still occurs in the latest version. Bug","color":"fef2c0","default":false,"description":null},{"id":16917029,"node_id":"MDU6TGFiZWwxNjkxNzAyOQ==","url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/labels/component:%20numpy._core","name":"component: numpy._core","color":"009800","default":false,"description":""},{"id":16917467,"node_id":"MDU6TGFiZWwxNjkxNzQ2Nw==","url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/labels/component:%20numpy.ma","name":"component: numpy.ma","color":"009800","default":false,"description":"masked arrays"}],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":7,"created_at":"2016-05-11T17:55:29Z","updated_at":"2024-10-27T07:13:25Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","active_lock_reason":null,"body":"Consider two masked arrays:

```
In [40]: a = np.ma.array([True] * 3, mask=[False] * 3)

In [41]: b = np.ma.array([True] * 3)
```

The only difference is that one has an all-false mask and the other has `mask=nomask`.  However, `ma.add.reduce` returns different values:

```
In [42]: np.ma.add.reduce(a)
Out[42]: True

In [43]: np.ma.add.reduce(b)
Out[43]: 3
```

ufuncs defined for `MaskedArrays` don't return `out` if it is supplied as a keyword, unlike normal ufuncs.

``` python
>>> a = np.ones(2)
>>> np.add([1,1], 2, out=a) is a
True
>>> np.ma.add([1,1], 2, out=a) is a

Bug","color":"fef2c0","default":false,"description":null},{"id":16917467,"node_id":"MDU6TGFiZWwxNjkxNzQ2Nw==","url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/labels/component:%20numpy.ma","name":"component: numpy.ma","color":"009800","default":false,"description":"masked arrays"}],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":3,"created_at":"2016-02-24T15:44:35Z","updated_at":"2024-10-27T07:21:24Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"NONE","active_lock_reason":null,"body":"Copying a Masked Array does not create a new MA with it's own fill value. Modifying the fill_value attribute on the destination array modifies the fill_value on the original source array.

The following code illustrates the issue:

`>>> import numpy as np, numpy.ma as ma`
`>>> a = ma.masked_values( np.array([9999., 0., 1., 2.]), 9999. )`
`>>> a.fill_value`
**9999.0**
`>>> b = ma.copy( a )`
`>>> ma.set_fill_value( b, -1. )`
`>>> a.fill_value`
**-1.0**
`>>>`

Maintenance","color":"fef2c0","default":false,"description":null}],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":8,"created_at":"2024-10-26T04:06:40Z","updated_at":"2024-10-29T06:00:26Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"NONE","active_lock_reason":null,"draft":false,"pull_request":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/pulls/27648","html_url":"https://github.com/numpy/numpy/pull/27648","diff_url":"https://github.com/numpy/numpy/pull/27648.diff","patch_url":"https://github.com/numpy/numpy/pull/27648.patch","merged_at":null},"body":"Fix the code style to our C-Style-Guide. More precisely, mainly do the following things:
 - **Rename Misleading Parameter Names**:
Rename parameters that could be misleading. For example, in line 590, the parameter `n_chars` in the function `buffer_memcpy` could be misunderstood as referring to the number of characters.
